rules.name: TODO and FIXME comments should be resolved
rules.description: TODO and FIXME comments indicate incomplete code that should be addressed. Leaving them in production code creates technical debt and can hide important incomplete work. This rule detects comments containing TODO, FIXME, XXX, or HACK markers.

detection_details.ast_query: Collect all comment nodes from the AST using tree-sitter comment queries
detection_details.pattern_match: Check if comment text contains case-insensitive patterns: TODO, FIXME, XXX, HACK, UNDONE, DEPRECATED
context_aware_whitelist: Test files (*.rs files with #[test] modules) - TODOs are often intentional test placeholders
Example code in examples/ directory
Generated code markers (// GENERATED, // DO NOT EDIT)
false_positive_whitelist: TODO in string literals: let msg = "TODO: implement this"
TODO in doc comments that describe planned API changes
FIXME in code that is intentionally left incomplete for later

false_positive_conditions: Generated code from build scripts
Code with //noinspection comment markers
TODO in comments that describe future API deprecation (not incomplete code)
rules.remediation: Either implement the missing functionality, remove the TODO comment if no longer needed, or create a GitHub issue to track the work and reference it in the comment.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the TODO/FIXME comment
2. Determine if the task is still relevant
3. If relevant: implement the fix or create an issue to track it
4. If obsolete: remove the comment
5. Update any associated documentation

SARIF_mapping.name: TODO and FIXME comments should be resolved

agent_semantics.summary: Unresolved TODO/FIXME comment found - indicates incomplete code
review_questions: Is this TODO still relevant to the current code?
Should this be a GitHub issue instead of an inline comment?
Is this in test code where TODOs are acceptable?
Has this been superseded by a later implementation?
semantic_chunks: TODO/FIXME comments are technical debt markers
In production code, these should be resolved or tracked in issue tracker
Test files are generally exempt from this rule
Fix: Implement the TODO, remove it, or create an issue
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Cannot safely autofix - requires human judgment on whether TODO is still valid

performance_budget.strategy: Simple string scan on comment tokens

rules.name: Empty nested blocks should be removed
rules.description: Empty blocks in if/else statements, loops, or closures add no logic but reduce code readability and can indicate forgotten implementation. This rule detects empty block statements that should be cleaned up.

detection_details.ast_query: Find block nodes in the AST that have no statements inside them
detection_details.pattern_match: Detect blocks with zero child statements, excluding function bodies
context_aware_whitelist: Function bodies - legitimate for function declarations without bodies (traits, extern)
Impl blocks with only method signatures
Trait definitions with only signatures
Struct definitions with no body (unit structs)
false_positive_whitelist: Empty loop bodies that intentionally spin: loop { std::hint::spin_loop(); }
Empty match arms that are explicitly unreachable: _ => unreachable!()
Conditional compilation blocks: #[cfg(test)] with empty module

exclusions: Trait and impl blocks with only signatures
false_positive_conditions: Empty loops used for synchronization (spin loops)
Empty match arms with explicit unreachable!()
Blocks in test code used as placeholders
rules.remediation: Remove empty blocks entirely, or add the intended logic inside the block.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the empty block
2. If the block serves no purpose: remove it entirely
3. If it should contain logic: add the intended implementation
4. Ensure any surrounding control flow still makes sense

SARIF_mapping.name: Empty nested blocks should be removed

agent_semantics.summary: Empty block detected - indicates incomplete implementation or dead code
review_questions: Is this block intentionally empty?
Should this block contain any logic?
Is this a placeholder for future code?
Should the surrounding control flow be simplified?
semantic_chunks: Empty blocks are usually unnecessary and reduce readability
May indicate forgotten implementation
Exception: Some empty blocks in traits/impls are legitimate
Fix: Remove the empty block or add intended logic
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Safe to remove empty blocks in most cases. Exclude function bodies, trait/impl blocks.

performance_budget.strategy: AST block node traversal with statement count

rules.name: Duplicate branches in if-else chain should be consolidated
rules.description: When multiple branches in an if-else chain have identical code, it indicates dead code and confusion about which condition is actually being checked. Only one branch will ever execute, making the conditions misleading.

detection_details.ast_query: Traverse if/else expression tree, collect all branch bodies and conditions
detection_details.pattern_match: Compare AST structure of consecutive branch bodies using tree-sitter capture
context_aware_whitelist: Early return pattern where conditions are intentionally different but body is same (code reuse pattern)
Logging/metrics branches with identical logic but different log levels
Branches containing only panic!() or unreachable!() with different messages
false_positive_whitelist: Braches that are semantically different but syntactically similar (e.g., a > 0 vs 0 < a)
Macro-expanded code that looks identical after macro expansion
Different literal values in otherwise identical code: return 1; vs return 2;

exclusions: Branches containing only panic!/unreachable! with different messages
false_positive_conditions: Code that looks identical but uses different captured variables
Brances with equivalent conditions expressed differently
Macro-generated code
rules.remediation: Consolidate duplicate branches into a single branch with a combined condition, or extract the common logic into a helper function.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the duplicate branch bodies
2. Analyze the conditions to determine if they can be combined (e.g., A || B)
3. If conditions are mutually exclusive: remove the redundant branch
4. If conditions can be combined: merge into single branch
5. Extract common logic to helper function if conditions cannot be combined

SARIF_mapping.name: Duplicate branches in if-else chain should be consolidated

agent_semantics.summary: Duplicate if-else branches detected - only one will ever execute
review_questions: Are the conditions mutually exclusive?
Should these conditions be combined with || ?
Is the duplicate code intentional (e.g., for documentation)?
Can the common logic be extracted to a helper function?
semantic_chunks: Duplicate branches mean only one condition can be true
This is dead code - one branch is unreachable
Often indicates confusion about which condition to check
Fix: Combine conditions with ||, or remove the dead branch
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Cannot safely autofix - requires understanding if branches are intentionally duplicated or conditions can be merged

performance_budget.strategy: AST traversal with structural comparison of branch bodies

rules.name: Empty statements should be removed
rules.description: Standalone semicolons or empty statements add no value and indicate incomplete work or copy-paste errors. In Rust, empty statements are almost always bugs.

detection_details.ast_query: Find expression_statement nodes that have no expression child
detection_details.pattern_match: Detect statements that consist only of a semicolon

rules.remediation: Remove the empty statement or replace with meaningful code.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the empty statement
2. Either remove it entirely
3. Or replace with meaningful expression if it was accidentally truncated

agent_semantics.summary: Empty statement detected - likely incomplete code
review_questions: Is this a truncated statement?
Should there be code between the semicolons?
semantic_chunks: Empty statements are almost always errors in Rust
Usually caused by accidental extra semicolons
Fix: Remove the empty statement
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Safe to remove empty statement - just delete the semicolon

rules.name: Redundant semicolons should be removed
rules.description: Extra semicolons after expressions or at the end of blocks are unnecessary in Rust and indicate sloppy code. This detects redundant semicolons that don't affect program semantics.

detection_details.ast_query: Find consecutive semicolons or semicolons in positions where they terminate nothing
detection_details.pattern_match: Detect patterns like let x = 5;; or fn() {};;
context_aware_whitelist: Semicolons in macro_rules that are required for the macro syntax
Semicolons in const/static definitions that are required
false_positive_whitelist: Semicolons at end of block expressions that return values
Semicolons in for loop heads: for i in 0..10; { ... } (if supported)

false_positive_conditions: Semicolons in extern crate declarations
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the redundant semicolon
2. Remove it
3. Ensure the code still compiles correctly

review_questions: Is this an extra semicolon that adds no value?
Does removing it change the code semantics?
semantic_chunks: Redundant semicolons add visual noise
Usually caused by typos oraccidental double semicolons
Fix: Remove the extra semicolon
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Safe to remove - extra semicolons in Rust are ignored by the compiler

rules.name: Wildcard patterns should not precede specific patterns in match
rules.description: When a wildcard (_) pattern appears before more specific patterns in a match arm's Or pattern, the subsequent patterns will never match. This is likely a bug where the programmer misunderstood pattern ordering.

detection_details.ast_query: Find match_arm nodes with or_pattern containing wildcard_pattern followed by other patterns
detection_details.pattern_match: Detect pattern structures like A | _ | B where the wildcard would catch everything
context_aware_whitelist: _ as the only pattern (correctly exhaustive)
Or patterns where wildcard is last: A | B | _ (correct ordering)
Guarded arms where order is intentional for readability
false_positive_whitelist: Match arms with different guards - wildcard may be before to catch specific guard combinations
Exhaustive matches with _ that is correctly positioned

exclusions: _ as the only pattern in an arm
A | B | _ where _ is correctly last
false_positive_conditions: Match arms with mutually exclusive guard conditions
When the semantics require catching remaining cases with wildcard
rules.remediation: Reorder the match arm patterns so that wildcard (_) appears last within an Or pattern.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the match arm with misplaced wildcard
2. Reorder patterns so _ comes last: A | B | _ instead of _ | A | B
3. Or remove the wildcard if the other patterns are already exhaustive

SARIF_mapping.name: Wildcard patterns should not precede specific patterns in match

agent_semantics.summary: Wildcard pattern in wrong position - subsequent patterns unreachable
review_questions: Is the wildcard correctly positioned in the Or pattern?
Should the _ be moved to the end of the pattern?
Are there guard conditions that make this ordering intentional?
semantic_chunks: In Rust match patterns, _ catches all remaining values
If _ appears before other patterns in an Or, those patterns are unreachable
This is a common bug from misunderstanding pattern order
Fix: Move _ to the end of the Or pattern
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Safe to reorder patterns - just move _ to the end of the Or pattern

rules.name: Function names should follow snake_case convention
rules.description: Rust convention is snake_case for function and method names. Using camelCase, PascalCase, or SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE violates the Rust API guidelines and reduces code readability for Rust developers.

detection_details.ast_query: Collect function_item and method_declaration nodes, extract identifier names
detection_details.pattern_match: Check if identifier matches snake_case pattern: ^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$
context_aware_whitelist: main function (convention allows)
Test functions: #[test] functions (often use camelCase for test names)
FFI declarations in extern blocks
Generated code with // GENERATED comment marker
Special main-like entry points
false_positive_whitelist: CamelCase in test function names (common practice)
Functions that must match external C APIs (FFI)
Iterator adapters andconsumers that match std library conventions

false_positive_conditions: Test functions using camelCase for readability
FFI functions that must match C naming
Trait required functions that must match external trait signatures
rules.remediation: Rename the function to use snake_case: myFunction -> my_function, calculateTotal -> calculate_total.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the non-snake_case function name
2. Convert to snake_case: remove capitals, insert underscores
3. Update all call sites to use the new name
4. If in a public API, consider maintaining backwards compatibility aliases

SARIF_mapping.name: Function names should follow snake_case convention

agent_semantics.summary: Non-snake_case function name detected - violates Rust conventions
review_questions: Is this a test function that should use camelCase?
Is this an FFI declaration that must match C naming?
Is this generated code that should not be modified?
Should this be renamed to snake_case?
semantic_chunks: Rust convention is snake_case for functions
Violating convention reduces readability for Rust developers
Exception: test functions, FFI, generated code
Fix: Rename to snake_case
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Cannot safely autofix - requires updating all call sites and potential API compatibility concerns

performance_budget.strategy: AST identifier collection + regex pattern match

rules.name: Functions containing only todo!() or unimplemented!() should be completed
rules.description: Functions whose body contains only a todo!(), unimplemented!(), or panic!() macro indicate incomplete implementation. These will panic at runtime if called, making them unsuitable for production code.

detection_details.ast_query: Find function_item nodes whose body is a block containing only a macro_invocation for todo!, unimplemented!, or panic!
detection_details.pattern_match: Detect function bodies that consist solely of panic-family macros
context_aware_whitelist: main() functions that intentionally panic on invalid state
Test setup functions that are meant to fail
Skeleton example binaries
Benchmark functions (often contain unimplemented!())
false_positive_whitelist: Functions with panic!() that include informative messages about why it's unreachable
Trait implementations where the method must exist but cannot be implemented
Never-type functions: fn foo() -> ! { unimplemented!() }

false_positive_conditions: unreachable!() in functions that truly should never be called
panic!() with location-specific error messages
Trait methods with default implementations
rules.remediation: Implement the function properly, or if intentionally stubbed, consider using a placeholder pattern that makes the intent clearer.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the stub function
2. Implement the intended functionality
3. If intentionally stubbed for later, consider adding a descriptive message
4. Ensure the function handles all expected cases properly

SARIF_mapping.name: Functions containing only todo!() or unimplemented!() should be completed

agent_semantics.summary: Stub function with todo!/unimplemented! detected - will panic if called
review_questions: Is this function intentionally stubbed for later implementation?
Should this function actually be implemented?
Is this in test/example code where stubs are acceptable?
Could this function be called in production?
semantic_chunks: todo!() and unimplemented!() will panic if called
Production code should not contain these unless truly unreachable
Stubs in tests/examples are generally acceptable
Fix: Implement the function or mark as #[fake] if intentional placeholder
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Cannot safely autofix - requires understanding the intended function behavior

rules.name: Redundant parenthesized expressions should be removed
rules.description: Unnecessary parentheses around expressions add visual noise and suggest the developer was unsure of operator precedence. This rule detects parenthesized expressions where the parentheses don't affect evaluation.

detection_details.ast_query: Find parenthesized_expression nodes and analyze if inner expression is simple
detection_details.pattern_match: Detect parentheses around simple expressions like literals, identifiers, or simple binary expressions where order is unambiguous
context_aware_whitelist: Parentheses in macro contexts where they are required
Parentheses in return statements for clarity: return (x);
Parentheses that affect async/await parsing
Parentheses in closure signatures: |x| (x + 1)
false_positive_whitelist: Parentheses that affect operator precedence
Parentheses in method calls: (foo.bar)()
Parentheses in tuple context

false_positive_conditions: Parentheses in complex expressions that clarify intent
Parentheses in match patterns
rules.remediation: Remove unnecessary parentheses while preserving any semantic meaning or precedence.
rules.fix_playbook:
1. Identify the parenthesized expression
2. Verify parentheses don't affect evaluation order
3. Remove the parentheses
4. Ensure code still compiles and has same behavior

SARIF_mapping.name: Redundant parenthesized expressions should be removed

agent_semantics.summary: Redundant parentheses detected - unnecessary visual noise
review_questions: Do these parentheses affect operator precedence?
Are they needed for macro parsing?
Do they clarify intent in a complex expression?
semantic_chunks: Unnecessary parentheses add visual noise
Usually safe to remove if they don't affect evaluation
Some contexts require parentheses (macros, returns)
Fix: Remove unnecessary parentheses
agent_semantics.autofix_guidance: Safe to remove when verified that parentheses don't affect precedence or parsing

performance_budget.strategy: AST parenthesized expression analysis with precedence check
